units.description: Greetings to Daily Life — master the core of everyday English.

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn everyday greetings and farewells used in English.

data.title: Meet the greetings
data.explanation: Read each greeting and its meaning.
data.example: Hello — A formal or casual way to greet someone.

data.secondary_text: A formal or casual way to greet someone.

data.question: It is 9:00 AM. You meet your teacher. What do you say?
data.explanation: 'Good morning' is used in the morning, before noon.

data.question: It is 3:00 PM. You see your friend. What do you say?
data.explanation: 'Good afternoon' is used between noon and 6 PM.

data.source_text: ___?__ you later! I have to go now.

data.title: Watch a real conversation
data.example: Ana: Hi! Good morning! | Ben: Good morning! How are you? | Ana: I'm fine, thank you. And you? | Ben: I'm great, thanks! See you later!

data.question: 'Hi' is more informal than 'Hello'.
data.explanation: 'Hi' is casual and friendly; 'Hello' is slightly more formal.

data.script: You arrive at school at 8 AM. Greet your teacher.

data.question: You are meeting your company's CEO for the first time.
options: Good afternoon, sir. It's a pleasure to meet you.

data.question: You are texting your best friend.
options: Good morning. I hope this message finds you well.
Heyyy! What are you up to? 😄
data.explanation: Casual texting with close friends is informal.

data.question: What is the best reply? (Finish the dialogue)
options: I'm doing well, thank you! How about you?
data.explanation: The natural response to 'How are you?' is to answer and ask back.

data.script: Good morning! You must be the new team member.

data.correct_answer: Hi! My name is Priya. It's great to meet you. I hope we can be good friends. See you in class tomorrow!
data.prompt: Write a short message (3–5 sentences) introducing yourself to a new classmate. Use at least 2 greetings or farewell expressions.

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn how to introduce yourself: name, age, nationality, and job.

data.title: Key vocabulary
data.explanation: Study these important words for introducing yourself.
data.example: name — What you are called. 'My name is Ana.'

data.secondary_text: What you are called. 'My name is Ana.'
How old you are. 'I am 25 years old.'
The country you are from. 'I am Indian.'
What you do for work. 'I am a teacher.'

data.title: Grammar: 'To be' — I am / My name is
data.explanation: Subject + am/is/are + [information]. Use 'am' only with 'I'. Use 'is' with he/she/it. Use 'are' with you/we/they
data.example: I am Carlos. | My name is Carlos. | I am 28 years old.

data.question: How does Carlos introduce his name correctly?
How does Priya say her age correctly?
data.explanation: Use 'I am + number + years old' to state your age.

data.title: A self-introduction
data.explanation: Read this dialogue. Notice how the person introduces themselves.
data.example: Sara: Hi! My name is Sara. I am from Brazil. | James: Nice to meet you, Sara! I am James. I am from the UK. | Sara: How old are you, James? | James: I am 24 years old. And you?

data.question: This person teaches students in a school.
This person cooks food in a restaurant.

data.script: Hello! My name is _____. I am _____ years old. I am from _____. I am a _____.

data.title: Negative introductions
data.explanation: To make 'to be' negative, add 'not': I am not, She is not, They are not.

data.prompt: Make it negative: "I am a student."
Make it negative: "She is from France."

data.correct_answer: Hi! My name is Meera. I am 26 years old. I am from Chennai, India. I am a software engineer. I love reading and cooking. I speak Tamil, Hindi, and English.

Clean your teeth with a toothbrush.
Eat the first meal of the day.

data.explanation: I/You/We/They + verb | He/She/It + verb+s. Add -s or -es to the verb when the subject is he, she, or it

data.question: She ___ up at 6 AM every day.

Which is the correct version?

units.title: Food, Taste & Ordering
units.description: Learn vocabulary for food and drink, express preferences, and practise ordering politely.

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn vocabulary for food and drink, express preferences, and practise ordering politely.

content.explanation: Learn the most common food and drink words organised by group.
Use these adjectives to describe how food tastes.
Before food words, choose 'a', 'an', or 'some' correctly.
content.tip: Uncountable = things you cannot count individually (water, rice, milk).

content.explanation: These phrases are used to order food and drink politely.

items.note: Polite way to say what you want
Waiter asks if you need more

items.phrase: Yes, that's all. / No, I'd also like ___.

items.note: Ask for the bill at the end

content.instruction: Repeat each phrase naturally. Focus on the polite tone.

phrases.text: I'd like an orange juice, please.
Can I have some rice, please?
This is absolutely delicious!
I don't like spicy food very much.
The bill, please.

content.question: Choose correct: 'I want ___ apple and ___ milk for breakfast.'
content.explanation: 'Apple' starts with vowel sound → an. 'Milk' is uncountable → some.

content.question: You eat a lemon. It makes your mouth close up. How does it taste?
content.explanation: Lemons taste sour — a sharp, acidic flavour.

content.question: You want to order soup at a restaurant. Which is the most polite way?
content.explanation: 'I'd like some soup, please' is polite and natural at a restaurant.

content.sentence: For lunch I have ___ egg, ___ bread, and ___ tomatoes.
content.explanation: an egg (vowel e-), some bread (uncountable), some tomatoes (plural countable).

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct polite ordering sentence.
content.correct_sentence: I'd like some soup and bread, please.
content.explanation: I'd like + some/a/an + [food], please.

content.instruction: Match each food to its most common taste.

content.context: Priya is ordering breakfast at a café for the first time.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Good morning! Welcome. What would you like?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: Good morning! Can I see the menu, please?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Of course! Here you are.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: Thank you. I'd like some eggs and toast, please.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Sure. Anything to drink?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: An orange juice, please. Is the juice fresh?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Yes, it's freshly squeezed!

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: Perfect. That's all, thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Great. I'll bring it in a few minutes.

comprehension_questions.question: What food does Priya order?
What drink does she order?
What does Priya ask about the juice?

content.instruction: Choose the correct sentence.
content.wrong_sentence: I'd like a rice and a egg, please.
options: I'd like a rice and a egg, please.
I'd like some rice and an egg, please.
I'd like some rice and a egg, please.
I'd like a rice and an egg, please.
content.explanation: Rice is uncountable → some rice. Egg starts with vowel → an egg.

content.scenario: You finish your meal at a restaurant. You want to pay and leave.
content.question: What do you say to the waiter?
content.explanation: 'The bill, please.' is the standard polite phrase to ask for your bill.

tasks.title: Speaking — Your food likes and dislikes

content.instruction: Speak for 40–50 seconds about your food preferences.
content.prompt: What food do you love? What do you not like? Describe how some foods taste.
content.example_response: I love spicy food — especially chicken curry. It's delicious! I also like fresh fruit. Mangoes are sweet and I eat them every day in summer. I don't like bitter food — I can't drink black coffee! I also don't eat salty snacks very much.

tasks.title: Speaking — Role play: order at a café

content.instruction: Imagine you are at a café. Order a meal and a drink politely. Ask one question about the food.
content.prompt: Waiter says: 'Good morning! What would you like today?'
content.example_response: Good morning! I'd like some eggs and toast, please. Can I also have an orange juice? Is the juice fresh? Great, thank you! That's all.

what_you_learned: Food vocabulary: staples, proteins, fruits, vegetables, drinks
Taste words: delicious, sweet, spicy, sour, salty, bitter, fresh, horrible
Articles: a (consonant), an (vowel sound), some (uncountable/plural)
Ordering: I'd like... / Can I have... please? / The bill, please.
Dialogue: café conversation — ordering food and drink
content.next_lesson_title: At the market — prices and quantities

content_manifest.grammar_point: Ask for prices, talk about quantities, and complete a market shopping conversation.

content.explanation: These are the most important phrases for buying things at a shop or market.
Choose 'how much' or 'how many' depending on the noun type.

rules.use_for: Uncountable nouns OR asking about price
examples: How much rice do you need?

rules.use_for: Countable nouns — things you can count 1, 2, 3
examples: How many oranges do you want?
How many bags do you need?

content.quick_test: Can you say 1 ___, 2 ___? If YES → How many. If NO → How much.

content.explanation: Use these to say how much of something you want to buy.

items.note: a kilo of tomatoes, a kilo of chicken
500 grams — half a kilo of rice
a litre of milk, a litre of oil
a bottle of water, a bottle of juice
a bag of rice, a bag of flour
large amount — a lot of vegetables

content.instruction: Repeat these market phrases naturally and clearly.

phrases.text: How much is this mango?
I'd like a kilo of tomatoes, please.
How many do you want?
That's too expensive for me.
How much is that in total?

content.question: '___ apples do you want?'
content.explanation: Apples are countable (1 apple, 2 apples) → How many.

content.question: '___ does a kilo of rice cost?'
content.explanation: For prices always use 'How much'. Rice is also uncountable.

content.question: You want to buy 12 eggs. What do you say?
options: I'd like a lot of eggs.
I'd like a bag of eggs.
content.explanation: A dozen = 12. 'A dozen eggs' is the natural expression.

tasks.title: Fill in: how much / how many

content.sentence: _____ milk is in the fridge? And _____ bananas are on the table?
content.explanation: Milk = uncountable → How much. Bananas = countable → How many.

content.sentence: I need ___ kilo of chicken and ___ bottle of oil for the recipe.
content.explanation: 'a kilo' (k = consonant), 'a bottle' (b = consonant) → both use 'a'.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct question to ask at the market.
content.explanation: How much + are + the [plural item]?

content.instruction: Match the most natural quantity expression to each product.

content.context: Ravi is shopping at a local market early in the morning.

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: Good morning! What do you need today, sir?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Good morning! How much are the tomatoes?

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: Fifty rupees a kilo. Very fresh — arrived this morning!

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Okay, good. I'll take a kilo of tomatoes, please.

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: Of course. Anything else?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Yes. How much are the onions?

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: Thirty rupees a kilo.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Half a kilo of onions, please. Do you have fresh ginger?

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: Yes, we do! Twenty rupees for 100 grams.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: That's a good price. I'll take some. How much is everything in total?

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: That's eighty rupees altogether.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Here you are. Thank you!

dialogue.speaker: Seller
dialogue.line: Thank you, sir! Come again!

comprehension_questions.question: How much does one kilo of tomatoes cost?
How much onion does Ravi buy?
How much does Ravi pay in total?

content.instruction: Which sentence is correct?
content.wrong_sentence: How many does this shirt cost?
options: How many does this shirt cost?
How much does this shirt cost?
How much is cost this shirt?
content.explanation: For prices always use 'How much'. Structure: How much + does + subject + cost?

content.scenario: At a market, the mango seller says the price is 200 rupees a kilo. You think this is very high.
content.question: What is the most natural thing to say?
options: That's too expensive. Do you have a cheaper price?
content.explanation: 'That's too expensive. Do you have a cheaper price?' is natural and polite.

content.instruction: Speak for 40 seconds. Describe going to a market. What did you buy and how much did it cost?
content.prompt: Tell me about the last time you went shopping for food. What did you buy?
content.example_response: I went to the market yesterday morning. I bought a kilo of tomatoes — they were fifty rupees. I also got a dozen eggs and a bottle of oil. I asked the seller how much the mangoes were but they were too expensive — two hundred rupees a kilo! So I didn't buy them.
key_phrases: a kilo of / a bottle of / a dozen

content.instruction: You are at the market. Ask prices for 3 items and buy at least 2 of them.
content.prompt: Seller says: 'Good morning! What are you looking for today?'
content.example_response: Good morning! How much are the apples? And how much is a bag of rice? The apples are a good price — I'll take a kilo, please. And a bag of rice. Do you have fresh milk? Great — a litre of milk too, please. How much is everything in total? Here you are!

what_you_learned: Shopping phrases: How much is/are? I'll take. That's too expensive.
How much (price/uncountable) vs How many (countable)
Quantities: a kilo, a litre, a bottle, a bag, a dozen, a few, a lot of
Dialogue: complete market shopping conversation
Real-world: negotiating and responding to prices

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn how to say and ask the time in English — o'clock, half past, quarter to/past.

content.explanation: In English we read time in a specific way. Learn these patterns.

tasks.title: AM and PM / Morning and evening

content.explanation: We can say AM/PM or use words like morning, afternoon, and evening.

items.note: Late — when it's dark and quiet

examples: My class is at 9 o'clock in the morning.
I have lunch at half past twelve.
The film starts at quarter past seven in the evening.

content.explanation: Use these phrases to ask and answer time questions.

items.note: Polite way to ask a stranger

content.instruction: Repeat each time expression. Listen carefully to the rhythm.

phrases.text: It's seven o'clock.
It's half past three.
It's quarter past nine.
It's quarter to eight.
What time is it, please?

content.question: The clock shows 3:30. How do you say this in English?
content.explanation: 3:30 = 30 minutes past 3 = half past three.

content.question: The clock shows 7:45. How do you say this?
content.explanation: 7:45 = 15 minutes before 8 = quarter to eight.

content.question: 'My train leaves at ten past six.' What time does the train leave?
content.explanation: Ten past six = 10 minutes after 6 = 6:10.

content.sentence: The meeting starts at _____ nine. (8:45)
content.explanation: 8:45 = 15 minutes before 9 = quarter to nine.

content.sentence: My class starts at _____ past seven. (7:20)
content.explanation: 7:20 = 20 minutes past 7 = twenty past seven.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct sentence about time.
content.correct_sentence: The film starts at half past seven.
content.explanation: at + half past + [hour] — 7:30.

tasks.title: Match the clock time to words

content.instruction: Match each digital time to its spoken English form.

content.context: Arun is running late and stops someone on the street to ask the time.

dialogue.speaker: Arun
dialogue.line: Excuse me! Do you have the time?
