comprehension_questions.question: What time is it when Arun asks?
What time does Arun's meeting start?
How long does it take to reach the office by auto?

content.instruction: Which sentence says the time correctly?
content.wrong_sentence: The train leaves at seven o'clock and half.
options: The train leaves at seven o'clock and half.
The train leaves at half seven o'clock.
The train leaves at half past seven.
The train leaves at seven and half past.
content.explanation: 7:30 = half past seven. 'Half past' always comes BEFORE the hour.

content.scenario: You don't have your phone. You want to know the time. You see a person nearby.
content.question: What is the most polite way to ask?
options: Excuse me, do you have the time?
content.explanation: 'Excuse me, do you have the time?' — polite, natural, and very common.

tasks.title: Speaking — Your daily schedule with times

content.instruction: Talk about your daily schedule using specific times — at least 5 activities.
content.prompt: What time do you do things in your day? Use o'clock, half past, quarter past/to.
content.example_response: I wake up at six o'clock every morning. I have breakfast at quarter past six. I leave the house at half past seven. My work starts at nine o'clock. I have lunch at one o'clock. I come home at half past six in the evening. I go to sleep at quarter to eleven.

what_you_learned: Time expressions: o'clock, half past, quarter past, quarter to
Minutes: ten past six (6:10), twenty to four (3:40)
Asking: What time is it? / Do you have the time?
Answering: It's half past three. / It's about nine o'clock.
AM/PM: in the morning, afternoon, evening, at night

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn days of the week, months, ordinal numbers for dates, and how to talk about schedules.

content.explanation: There are 7 days in a week. Always write them with a capital letter in English.

items.note: First working day of the week
Middle of the week — tricky spelling!
Weekend — last day of the week

content.tip: Weekdays = Monday to Friday. Weekend = Saturday and Sunday.

content.explanation: There are 12 months. Always capitalise them in English.
We use ordinal numbers (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th...) to say dates in English.
content.rule: Most ordinals: add -th (four→fourth). Special: 1st first, 2nd second, 3rd third.

tasks.title: Prepositions of time: on / in / at

content.explanation: Choose the correct preposition for days, months, and times.

content.instruction: Repeat these date expressions clearly.

phrases.text: My birthday is on the fifteenth of August.
The meeting is on Monday.
We go on holiday in July.
The exam is on the third of November.
What date is it today?

content.question: My birthday is ___ March.
content.explanation: Months use 'in'. In March, in July, in December.

content.question: I have a class ___ Monday.
content.explanation: Days use 'on'. On Monday, on Friday, on the weekend.

tasks.title: How do you say this date?

content.question: How do you say '3rd April' in English?
content.explanation: Dates: the + ordinal number + of + month. 'The third of April'.

content.sentence: We have a holiday ___ December. My family visits ___ the 25th. We celebrate ___ night.
content.explanation: in December (month), on the 25th (date), at night (specific time expression).

content.sentence: Independence Day in India is ___ the fifteenth of August.
content.explanation: Dates use 'on' — on the fifteenth of August.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct sentence about a date.
content.correct_sentence: My birthday is the twenty-first of June.
content.explanation: My birthday + is + the + ordinal + of + month.

content.instruction: Match the number to the correct spoken ordinal.

content.context: Two colleagues Divya and Kiran are arranging a meeting.

dialogue.speaker: Divya
dialogue.line: Kiran, when are you free to meet this week?

dialogue.speaker: Kiran
dialogue.line: Let me check. Tuesday is busy — I have a presentation.

dialogue.speaker: Divya
dialogue.line: What about Wednesday?

dialogue.speaker: Kiran
dialogue.line: Wednesday morning is free! What time works for you?

dialogue.speaker: Divya
dialogue.line: Is ten o'clock okay?

dialogue.speaker: Kiran
dialogue.line: Perfect. And what date is that? The fourteenth?

dialogue.speaker: Divya
dialogue.line: Yes, Wednesday the fourteenth of March.

dialogue.speaker: Kiran
dialogue.line: Great, I'll put it in my calendar. See you then!

comprehension_questions.question: Why can't Kiran meet on Tuesday?
What time do they decide to meet?
What is the full date of the meeting?

content.instruction: Choose the correct sentence.
content.wrong_sentence: My exam is in Monday on November.
options: My exam is in Monday on November.
My exam is on Monday in November.
My exam is at Monday in November.
My exam is on Monday at November.
content.explanation: Days use 'on' (on Monday). Months use 'in' (in November).

content.scenario: Someone asks you: 'When is your birthday?'
content.question: Which is the most natural and correct answer?
options: My birthday is on the fifteenth of August.
content.explanation: My birthday is + on + the + ordinal + of + month.

tasks.title: Speaking — Your week and important dates

content.instruction: Talk about your week. What do you do on which days? Also mention one important date.
content.prompt: Tell me about your weekly schedule and one special date coming up.
content.example_response: On Monday and Tuesday I go to work. Wednesday is my favourite day because I finish early. On Thursday evenings I go to the gym. Fridays are busy — I have meetings. At the weekend I relax. My sister's birthday is on the twelfth of May — I'm very excited!

what_you_learned: Days: Monday to Sunday (always capital letters)
Months: January to December (always capital letters)
Ordinal numbers: first, second, third, fourth... twentieth, twenty-first
Saying dates: the + ordinal + of + month (the fifth of March)
Prepositions: on (days/dates), in (months/years), at (times/night)

content_manifest.grammar_point: Describe today's weather and learn to talk about what is happening right now.

content.explanation: These are the most common words to describe the weather.

items.note: Wet, sticky air — common in Chennai!
Strong wind and heavy rain together

content.explanation: We use the present continuous to talk about what is happening RIGHT NOW.
content.negative: Subject + am/is/are + NOT + verb-ing: It is not raining. / She isn't working.
content.question: Am/Is/Are + Subject + verb-ing? Is it raining? / Are you working?

content.explanation: Know when to use each tense — this is very important!

comparison.use: Habits and routines — always, usually, every day
examples: I walk to work every day.
It rains a lot in July.

comparison.use: Happening right now — at this moment
examples: I am walking to work now.

content.instruction: Repeat naturally. Notice how we contract is → 's in speech.

phrases.text: It's raining heavily outside.
The sun is shining and it's very hot.
It's not raining now — it's cloudy.
What's the weather like today?
I'm staying inside because it's stormy.

content.question: Choose the correct sentence for RIGHT NOW: 'She ___ her homework.'
content.explanation: Right now = present continuous. She + is + doing (verb-ing).

content.question: 'Look! It ___ !' (You can see rain out of the window right now.)
content.explanation: 'Look!' signals something happening RIGHT NOW → present continuous. It is raining.

content.question: It is very hot and the air feels wet and sticky. What weather is this?
content.explanation: Humid = hot and wet air — very common in coastal cities like Chennai.

content.sentence: Right now I ___ (sit) at my desk and I ___ (study) English.
content.explanation: Right now = present continuous. I + am + verb-ing.

tasks.title: Fill in: simple present or continuous?

content.sentence: It usually ___ (rain) a lot in June, but today it ___ (shine).
content.explanation: Usually → simple present (rains). Today/right now → present continuous (is shining).

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct present continuous sentence.
content.explanation: It + is + verb-ing + [adverb] + [place].

content.instruction: Match the weather to the most likely activity.

pairs.answer: People are going to the beach.

content.context: Two friends Anand and Nisha are chatting by phone on a rainy day.

dialogue.speaker: Anand
dialogue.line: Hey Nisha! What's the weather like in Chennai today?

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Oh, it's terrible! It's raining very heavily right now.

dialogue.speaker: Anand
dialogue.line: Are you at home?

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Yes! I'm sitting by the window and watching the rain. What about Bangalore?

dialogue.speaker: Anand
dialogue.line: It's a bit cloudy here but it isn't raining. It's actually quite cool.

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Lucky you! I can't go out today — the roads are flooding.

dialogue.speaker: Anand
dialogue.line: Stay safe! Are you doing anything at home?

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: I'm cooking and reading. A perfect rainy day!

comprehension_questions.question: What is the weather like in Chennai?
What is Nisha doing right now?
options: Sitting by the window watching the rain

comprehension_questions.question: What is the weather like in Bangalore?

content.instruction: Which sentence is correct for something happening right now?
content.wrong_sentence: Look! The children play in the garden.
options: Look! The children play in the garden.
Look! The children are playing in the garden.
Look! The children playing in the garden.
Look! The children is playing in the garden.
content.explanation: 'Look!' signals right now → present continuous. They + are + playing.

content.scenario: Your friend calls you and asks 'What are you doing?' You are at home cooking dinner.
content.question: What is the most natural reply?
options: I am cooking dinner right now.
content.explanation: What are you doing? = right now → present continuous. I am cooking.

tasks.title: Speaking — Describe the weather and what you're doing

content.instruction: Speak for 45 seconds. Describe today's weather and what you are doing right now because of it.
content.prompt: What is the weather like today? What are you doing? What are people around you doing?
content.example_response: Today the weather in Chennai is very hot and humid. It's not raining but the sky is a bit cloudy. Right now I am sitting inside with the fan on because it's too hot to go outside. My mother is cooking in the kitchen and my brother is watching TV. Usually on Sundays we go for a walk but today we're all staying inside.

what_you_learned: Weather: sunny, cloudy, rainy, windy, hot, cold, humid, foggy, stormy
Present continuous: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing
Use: things happening RIGHT NOW
Negative: isn't/aren't + verb-ing
Question: Is it raining? / What are you doing?
Key difference: simple present (habits) vs present continuous (now)

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn transport vocabulary, how to ask for directions, and use 'can' for ability and requests.

content.explanation: Learn the most common ways to travel in English.

items.note: Take the bus / catch the bus
Take the train / catch the metro
Very common in India — take an auto
Book a taxi / call a cab
Drive a car / go by car

content.explanation: Use 'can' to talk about ability (what is possible) and to make polite requests.

examples: Can you tell me the way?

content.explanation: Use these phrases when using transport or asking about travel.

items.note: Tell someone where to leave the bus/metro
Ask if there's a non-stop route

content.instruction: Repeat these travel phrases clearly.

phrases.text: Can you tell me how to get to the station?
How long does it take by bus?
It takes about twenty minutes.
I can't drive — I always take the metro.
Get off at the third stop.

content.question: She never drives — she didn't learn. 'She ___ drive.'
content.explanation: She never learned to drive → she can't drive. Negative ability.

content.question: Which sentence is correct?
options: She can to ride a bike.
content.explanation: Can + base verb (no -s, no -ing, no 'to'). She can ride. ✓

content.question: You want to travel from Chennai to Delhi. Which transport do you use?
content.explanation: Long distances between cities = train or flight. Auto and bicycle are for short local trips.

content.sentence: My father ___ drive but I ___. I always take the bus.
content.explanation: Father drives → can. I always take the bus (don't drive) → can't.

content.sentence: Excuse me, how ___ does it ___ to get to the airport by metro?
content.explanation: How long does it take? = asking about time to travel.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a polite request.
content.correct_sentence: Can you tell me how to get to the bus station?
content.explanation: Can + you + tell + me + how + to + get to + [place]?

content.instruction: Match each transport with the correct verb.

content.context: Sara is a tourist in Chennai. She asks a local person for help.

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: Excuse me! Can you help me, please?

dialogue.speaker: Local
dialogue.line: Of course! What do you need?

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: How do I get to the Marina Beach from here?

dialogue.speaker: Local
dialogue.line: It's not very far. You can take bus number 21C, or you can take an auto.

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: How long does it take by bus?

dialogue.speaker: Local
dialogue.line: About fifteen minutes. Get off at the beach stop — you'll see the sea!

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: And by auto?

dialogue.speaker: Local
dialogue.line: Only ten minutes, but it's more expensive.

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: Okay, I'll take the bus. Can I buy a ticket on the bus?

dialogue.speaker: Local
dialogue.line: Yes, you can pay the conductor on the bus.

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: Perfect! Thank you so much!

dialogue.speaker: Local
dialogue.line: You're welcome! Enjoy the beach!

comprehension_questions.question: Where does Sara want to go?
How long does the bus take?
Which transport does Sara choose?

content.instruction: Which sentence is correct?
content.wrong_sentence: Can you to tell me where is the bus stop?
options: Can you to tell me where is the bus stop?
Can you tell me where the bus stop is?
Can you tell me where is bus stop?
You can tell me where the bus stop is?
content.explanation: No 'to' after can. Embedded question: where + subject + verb (not inverted).

content.scenario: You are in a new city. You need to get to the railway station. You see a person on the street.
content.question: Which is the most complete and polite thing to say?
options: Excuse me, can you tell me how to get to the railway station?
content.explanation: Full polite sentence: Excuse me + Can you + tell me + how to get to + [place]?

tasks.title: Speaking — How do you get around?

content.instruction: Talk for 45 seconds about how you travel in your daily life. Use can/can't.
content.prompt: How do you usually travel to work or school? Can you drive? What transport do you like?
content.example_response: I usually travel to work by bus. I can't drive — I never learned. My commute takes about thirty minutes. I take bus number 21 and get off at the last stop. I don't like taking autos because they're expensive. On weekends I sometimes walk to the market — it's only ten minutes on foot. I'd like to learn to drive one day!

what_you_learned: Transport: bus, train, metro, auto, taxi, bike, car, flight, on foot
Verbs: drive (car), ride (bike), take (bus/train), fly (plane), walk
Can/can't: ability (I can drive), requests (Can you help me?)
Can + base verb ONLY — no -s, no -to, no -ing
Travel questions: How do I get to? / How long does it take? / How far is it?

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn to have a full restaurant conversation — ordering, asking questions, and paying.

content.explanation: Learn the essential words and phrases for eating at a restaurant.

items.note: The list of food and prices
Small dish at the beginning of the meal
The biggest dish of the meal
Sweet dish at the end — cake, ice cream
Table for two / three / four people
The paper showing what you owe
Extra money you give for good service

content.explanation: These are the most useful phrases for making polite requests.

items.phrase: Could we have the menu, please?

items.note: Ask the waiter for their best dish

items.phrase: Could we have the bill, please?
items.note: Ask for the bill at the end

content.explanation: 'Could' is more polite than 'can' for requests. Both are correct.

comparison.more_polite: Could I have some water, please?
Could you bring the bill, please?
Could I see the menu, please?

content.rule: Could + subject + base verb + please? → always polite, always correct at a restaurant.
replies_from_waiter: I'm sorry, we don't have that today.

content.instruction: Repeat these restaurant phrases with a polite, clear tone.

phrases.text: Excuse me! Could I see the menu, please?
I'd like the grilled fish, please.
What do you recommend?
Could we have some more water, please?
Could we have the bill, please?

content.question: The small dish you eat BEFORE the main meal is called a ___.
content.explanation: A starter (or appetiser) is eaten before the main course.

content.question: You want more water. Which is the most polite thing to say to the waiter?
options: Could you bring some more water, please?
content.explanation: 'Could you bring' + please = most polite. Always use at a restaurant.

content.question: You have finished your meal and want to pay. What do you say?
options: Could we have the bill, please?
Is there a table for two?
content.explanation: 'Could we have the bill, please?' — the standard phrase to ask for the bill.

content.sentence: Excuse me! _____ I see the menu? And _____ you tell me what today's special is?
content.explanation: Both can and could work — but 'could' is more polite in a restaurant context.

content.sentence: We have a ___ at 8pm for a table for four people.
content.explanation: A reservation = a booking — you call in advance to keep a table.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct polite request.
content.correct_sentence: Could you bring some more bread, please?
content.explanation: Could + you + bring + some + more + [item] + please?

tasks.title: Match the situation to the phrase

content.instruction: Match each restaurant situation to the correct phrase.

pairs.prompt: You arrive and want to sit down
You want to see the food list
pairs.answer: Could I see the menu, please?

pairs.prompt: You don't know what to order
You want to pay and leave
pairs.answer: Could we have the bill, please?

pairs.prompt: You want to get the waiter's attention

content.context: Arun and his friend go to a restaurant for dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Good evening! Do you have a reservation?

dialogue.speaker: Arun
dialogue.line: Yes, I have a booking for two. The name is Arun.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Of course! This way, please. Here's your table.

dialogue.speaker: Arun
dialogue.line: Thank you. Could we see the menu, please?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Certainly. Here you are. Can I get you any drinks to start?

dialogue.speaker: Arun
dialogue.line: Could we have two glasses of water, please?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Of course. Are you ready to order?

dialogue.speaker: Arun
dialogue.line: Not yet. What do you recommend for the main course?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: The grilled fish is very popular tonight. It's delicious!

dialogue.speaker: Arun
dialogue.line: Sounds good! I'd like the grilled fish, please.

dialogue.speaker: Friend
dialogue.line: And I'll have the pasta with vegetables.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Excellent choices! I'll bring your food shortly.

dialogue.speaker: Arun
dialogue.line: (Later) Excuse me! Could we have the bill, please?

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: Of course! I hope you enjoyed your meal.

dialogue.speaker: Arun
dialogue.line: It was absolutely delicious. Thank you!

comprehension_questions.question: What does Arun ask for first?
What does the waiter recommend?
What does Arun's friend order?

content.instruction: Which is the correct polite request?
content.wrong_sentence: Could you to bring the bill please?
options: Could you to bring the bill please?
Could you bringing the bill please?
Could you bring the bill, please?
Could you brought the bill please?
content.explanation: Could + you + BASE verb (no 'to', no -ing, no past form). Could you bring ✓

content.scenario: You are at a restaurant. The waiter brings the wrong dish — you ordered fish but they brought chicken.
content.question: What is the most polite way to handle this?
options: This is wrong! Take it back!
Excuse me, I think there's a mistake. I ordered the fish, not the chicken.
content.explanation: Polite complaint: Excuse me + I think there's a mistake + explain calmly. Never be rude.

content.instruction: Play BOTH parts — customer and waiter. Have a full restaurant conversation covering: arrival, menu, ordering, and bill.
content.prompt: Start as a customer entering a restaurant. Have a complete conversation.
content.example_response: Good evening! A table for two, please. Could I see the menu? Thank you. Excuse me, what do you recommend? The grilled fish sounds good. I'd like the grilled fish and my friend will have the pasta. Could we also have some water? (Later) Excuse me! Could we have the bill please? Thank you — it was absolutely delicious!

what_you_learned: Restaurant words: menu, starter, main course, dessert, bill, tip, reservation
Polite requests: Could I have ___? / Could you bring ___?
Could vs Can: both correct — could is more formal/polite
Could + base verb (no 'to', no -ing)
Full restaurant flow: arrive → menu → order → eat → bill → pay
Dialogue: complete restaurant interaction

content_manifest.grammar_point: Review all Unit 2 topics: food, shopping, time, dates, weather, transport, and restaurant.

content.explanation: Let's review everything from Unit 2 before your final test.

summary.topic: Food & drink — a/an/some, taste words, ordering
Market — how much/many, quantities (kilo, litre, dozen)
Time — o'clock, half past, quarter past/to
Weather — present continuous (is raining, am sitting)
Restaurant — could for requests, full conversation

content.question: Choose the correct sentence: 'For breakfast I have ___ egg and ___ bread.'
content.explanation: an egg (vowel e), some bread (uncountable) ✓

content.question: '___ rice do you want?' and '___ apples should I buy?'
content.explanation: Rice = uncountable → How much. Apples = countable → How many.

content.question: The clock shows 8:45. How do you say this?
content.explanation: 8:45 = 15 minutes before 9 = quarter to nine.

content.question: My birthday is ___ the 10th ___ April.
content.explanation: Dates → on. Months → in. On the 10th of April. ✓

content.question: Look outside! It ___ and the children ___ inside.
content.explanation: Both actions are happening right now → both need present continuous.

content.question: Which sentence is grammatically correct?
options: She can to drive a car.
content.explanation: Can + base verb ONLY. No -s, no 'to', no -ing. She can drive. ✓

content.question: You are at a restaurant and want more water. Which is MOST polite?
options: Could you bring some more water, please?
content.explanation: Could + you + bring + please = most polite restaurant request.

content.sentence: The meeting is ___ Wednesday ___ quarter past ten ___ the morning.
content.explanation: on Wednesday (day), at quarter past ten (time), in the morning (part of day).

content.sentence: Right now my sister ___ (cook) dinner and my brother ___ (watch) TV.
content.explanation: Right now = present continuous. She is cooking. He is watching.

content.sentence: I ___ drive so I always ___ the bus to work. It ___ about 20 minutes.
content.explanation: can't drive (no ability), take the bus (correct verb), takes 20 minutes (travel time).

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct sentence.
content.correct_sentence: The film starts at half past seven.
content.explanation: at + half past + hour = 7:30.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct polite restaurant request.
content.correct_sentence: Could we have the bill, please?
content.explanation: Could + we + have + the + [item] + please?

content.instruction: This paragraph has 3 mistakes. Which version corrects them all?
content.wrong_text: Today it is rain and I can't going outside. I am eat breakfast at home — some rice and a egg. My meeting is in Monday at nine o'clock.
options: Today it is raining and I can't go outside. I am eating breakfast at home — some rice and an egg. My meeting is on Monday at nine o'clock.
Today it is raining and I can't going outside. I am eating breakfast — some rice and an egg. My meeting is in Monday.
Today it rains and I can't go outside. I eat breakfast — some rice and a egg. My meeting is on Monday.
Today it is rain and I can't go outside. I am eating — some rice and an egg. My meeting is at Monday.
content.explanation: 3 errors: 'rain'→'raining' (present continuous), 'can't going'→'can't go' (can + base verb), 'a egg'→'an egg' (vowel), 'in Monday'→'on Monday' (day).

tasks.title: Review — Match grammar rule to example

content.instruction: Match each sentence to its grammar rule.

pairs.prompt: My birthday is on the 5th of June.

content.context: Meera is having a busy morning. She talks to her colleague Raj at work.

dialogue.speaker: Raj
dialogue.line: Good morning, Meera! You look tired. Are you okay?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: Morning, Raj! Yes, I'm fine — just a busy morning!

dialogue.speaker: Raj
dialogue.line: What happened?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: Well, I woke up at half past six. It was raining heavily, so I couldn't take my bike.

dialogue.speaker: Raj
dialogue.line: Oh no! How did you get here?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: I took the bus. It took forty minutes — usually it takes twenty!

dialogue.speaker: Raj
dialogue.line: That's a long time. Did you have breakfast?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: Just an apple and some tea. I didn't have time to cook.

dialogue.speaker: Raj
dialogue.line: Do you want to go to the café downstairs at lunch?

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: Yes please! What time is it now?

dialogue.speaker: Raj
dialogue.line: It's quarter to ten. Lunch is at one o'clock — three hours to go!

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: Perfect. I'll have a proper meal then. I'm starving!

comprehension_questions.question: Why couldn't Meera take her bike?
How long did the bus take?
What time does the dialogue happen?

tasks.title: Final speaking — Part 1: Your day so far

content.instruction: Speak for 60–75 seconds. Talk about your morning today using everything you learned in Unit 2.
content.prompt: Describe your morning. Include: what time you woke up, the weather, what you ate, how you travelled, and what you are doing now.
content.example_response: I woke up at half past six this morning. The weather was hot and sunny — not a cloud in the sky! For breakfast I had some eggs, toast, and an orange juice. I took the bus to work — it took about twenty-five minutes. I arrived at quarter to nine. Right now I am sitting at my desk and working. I'm also planning lunch — I'd like to go to a restaurant near the office at one o'clock.

tasks.title: Final speaking — Part 2: Restaurant role play

content.instruction: Have a complete restaurant conversation. Cover: arriving, ordering food and drink, asking a question, and paying.
content.prompt: You arrive at a restaurant for lunch with a friend. Start the conversation.
content.example_response: Good afternoon! A table for two, please. Thank you. Could we see the menu? Excuse me, what do you recommend for the main course? The chicken curry sounds delicious — I'll have that, please. And could we have two glasses of water? My friend will have the vegetable pasta. (Later) Excuse me! Could we have the bill, please? Thank you, everything was absolutely delicious!

content.congratulations: Excellent! You have completed Unit 2. You can now talk about food, go shopping, tell the time and date, describe weather, use transport, and have a full restaurant conversation — all in English!
all_grammar_covered: Time: o'clock, half past, quarter past, quarter to, ___ past/to
content.next_unit_title: Unit 3 — My world: body, health, clothes & feelings

units.title: Daily Life & Surroundings
units.description: Talk about your body, clothes, feelings, and the home you live in.

lessons.title: The body & health — I have a headache

metadata.lesson_title: The body & health — I have a headache

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn body part vocabulary and how to describe health problems and symptoms.

content.explanation: Learn the most important body part words in English.
In English, we usually say 'I have a + problem' OR 'My ___ hurts' to describe pain.
Use these words to describe how you feel when you are sick.

content.instruction: Repeat clearly. These phrases are very important for everyday life.

phrases.text: I have a bad headache.
My throat is very sore.
I feel dizzy and weak.
I have a high fever.
I don't feel well today.

content.question: Your head is painful and heavy. What do you say?
content.explanation: Pain in the head = a headache. 'I have a headache.'

content.question: Choose the correct article: 'I have ___ allergy to pollen.'
content.explanation: 'Allergy' starts with vowel 'a' → use 'an'. An allergy. ✓

content.question: Which sentence is correct for talking about knee pain?
content.explanation: For body parts without -ache form, use: My + [body part] + hurts.

content.sentence: I can't eat anything — I have a terrible ___ache in my tooth.
content.explanation: Can't eat + pain in tooth = toothache.

content.sentence: I had a fever last night but today I feel much ___.
content.explanation: After being sick and improving → I feel better.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct sentence about a health problem.
content.correct_sentence: I have a cold and a sore throat.
content.explanation: I have + a + [problem] + and + a + [problem].

content.instruction: Match each symptom description to the correct health problem.

pairs.prompt: Your nose is running and you sneeze a lot
You keep coughing every few minutes
The room feels like it's spinning

content.context: Priya looks unwell at work. Her colleague Sanjay asks what's wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Sanjay
dialogue.line: Priya, are you okay? You look pale.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: Not really. I don't feel very well today.

dialogue.speaker: Sanjay
dialogue.line: What's wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: I have a terrible headache and my throat is really sore.

dialogue.speaker: Sanjay
dialogue.line: Oh no! Do you have a fever?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: I think so. I feel hot and I feel a bit dizzy too.

dialogue.speaker: Sanjay
dialogue.line: You should go home and rest. Did you take any medicine?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: Not yet. I don't have any tablets with me.

dialogue.speaker: Sanjay
dialogue.line: I have some paracetamol — here, take these.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.line: Thank you so much, Sanjay. You're very kind.

dialogue.speaker: Sanjay
dialogue.line: Rest well! I hope you feel better soon.

comprehension_questions.question: What two health problems does Priya have?
What does Sanjay give Priya?
What does Sanjay say Priya should do?

content.instruction: Which sentence correctly describes a health problem?
content.wrong_sentence: I have headache and my stomach hurt.
options: I have headache and my stomach hurt.
I have a headache and my stomach hurts.
I have a headache and my stomach hurt.
I have headache and my stomach hurts.
content.explanation: Need article 'a' before headache. 'My stomach hurts' (singular → hurts not hurt).

content.scenario: You call your office to say you cannot come to work because you are sick.
content.question: What is the most natural thing to say?
options: Hello, I am not come today.
Hello, I am sorry — I don't feel well. I have a fever and a bad headache.
Hello, my body is problem today.
content.explanation: Clear, polite, and uses correct health vocabulary: I don't feel well / I have a fever.

content.instruction: Speak for 40–50 seconds. Describe a time when you felt sick. What was wrong? How did you feel? What did you do?
content.prompt: Tell me about a time you were not feeling well. What health problem did you have?
content.example_response: Last week I felt terrible. I had a high fever and a really bad headache. My throat was sore too and I had a cough. I felt very weak and dizzy. I couldn't go to work. I stayed at home, drank a lot of water, and took some medicine. After two days I felt much better. I think I had a bad cold.

what_you_learned: Body parts: head, face, eye, ear, nose, throat, back, stomach, knee, foot etc.
I have a ___ache: headache, stomachache, toothache, backache
I have a/an ___: cold, fever, cough, sore throat, allergy
My ___ hurts: for body parts without -ache form
Feeling words: sick, terrible, dizzy, weak, tired, better, fine

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn to have a complete doctor's appointment conversation and describe how long you have been sick.

content.explanation: These words and phrases are essential for a visit to the doctor.

items.note: A booked time to see the doctor
What you take to get better
Doctor says: stay home and rest
Signs of an illness — pain, fever, cough
When the doctor checks your body

content.explanation: The doctor always asks 'how long' you have been sick. Use these patterns.

patterns.note: From a specific point in time

doctor_questions.question: How long have you had this?
Does it hurt when I do this?
Are you allergic to any medicine?
doctor_questions.meaning: Do any medicines cause a bad reaction?

content.explanation: Doctors use 'should' to give advice. Learn these common phrases.

advice_phrases.phrase: You should drink lots of water.
You should take this medicine twice a day.
You should come back in three days.

content.grammar_note: Should + base verb (no -s, no 'to'). You should rest. ✓  You should to rest. ✗

content.instruction: Repeat these doctor's appointment phrases clearly.

phrases.text: I've had a headache for three days.
Where exactly does it hurt?
You should rest and drink plenty of water.
I'll write you a prescription.
I'm not allergic to any medicine.

content.question: 'I have had a fever ___ Monday.' — Choose the correct word.
content.explanation: Monday is a specific point in time → use 'since'. I have had a fever since Monday.

content.question: 'My back has been painful ___ two weeks.'
content.explanation: 'Two weeks' is a duration/period of time → use 'for'. For two weeks.

content.question: The doctor asks 'What's the matter?' — What does this mean?
content.explanation: 'What's the matter?' = 'What is wrong?' — a common phrase doctors and friends use.

content.sentence: I have had a cough ___ three days — it started ___ last Tuesday.
content.explanation: Three days = duration → for. Last Tuesday = specific time → since.

content.sentence: You ___ rest at home and you ___ go to work for two days.
content.explanation: Rest = positive advice → should. Don't go to work = negative advice → shouldn't.

content.instruction: Rearrange to tell the doctor how long you have been sick.
content.correct_sentence: I have had a sore throat since yesterday.
content.explanation: I have had + a + [problem] + since + [point in time].

tasks.title: Match doctor's question to patient's answer

content.instruction: Match each doctor's question with the best patient reply.

pairs.answer: I have a bad headache and a fever.

pairs.prompt: How long have you had this?
Are you allergic to any medicine?
pairs.answer: No, I'm not allergic to anything.
You should rest and drink lots of water.

content.context: Ravi visits the doctor after feeling unwell for a few days.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Good morning! Please sit down. What's the matter?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Good morning, Doctor. I don't feel well. I have a high fever and a very bad headache.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: I see. How long have you had these symptoms?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: For about three days — since Monday.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Do you have a cough or sore throat?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Yes, my throat is a bit sore. And I feel very tired and weak.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Okay. Let me examine you. Open your mouth, please. Say 'Ah'.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Aaaah.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Your throat is a little inflamed. Are you allergic to any medicine?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: No, I'm not.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Good. I'll prescribe you some tablets. Take one three times a day with food.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Should I rest at home?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Yes, you should rest for two days. Drink lots of water and avoid cold food.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.line: Thank you, Doctor. When should I come back?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Come back in three days if you don't feel better. Take care!

comprehension_questions.question: How long has Ravi had his symptoms?
What does the doctor prescribe?
How many times a day should Ravi take the tablets?

content.instruction: Which sentence correctly uses should?
content.wrong_sentence: You should to rest and you should to drink water.
options: You should to rest and you should to drink water.
You should rest and you should drink water.
You should resting and you should drinking water.
You should rests and you should drinks water.
content.explanation: Should + BASE verb only. No 'to', no -ing, no -s. You should rest. ✓

content.scenario: The doctor asks: 'How long have you had this stomachache?' Your pain started two days ago.
content.question: Which is the most accurate and natural reply?
content.explanation: Two days = a period of time → use 'for'. For two days. ✓

content.instruction: Role play a doctor's appointment. Play BOTH the doctor and the patient. Cover: greeting, symptoms, how long, doctor's advice.
content.prompt: You have a bad cold and a cough for four days. Visit the doctor.
content.example_response: Doctor: Good morning! What's the matter? Patient: Good morning. I have a bad cold and a terrible cough. Doctor: How long have you had these symptoms? Patient: For four days — since last Thursday. Doctor: Do you have a fever? Patient: Yes, a small one. I also feel very tired. Doctor: You should rest at home and drink lots of warm water. Take these tablets twice a day. Patient: Should I avoid cold food? Doctor: Yes, you shouldn't eat ice cream or cold drinks. Come back in three days if you're not better.

what_you_learned: Doctor vocab: appointment, prescription, medicine, tablet, symptoms, examination
Duration: for + period (for three days) / since + specific time (since Monday)
Doctor's questions: What's the matter? / How long? / Where does it hurt? / Any allergies?
Doctor's advice: should/shouldn't + base verb
Should structure: should + BASE verb — no 'to', no -ing, no -s

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn clothing vocabulary, colours, and how to describe what people are wearing.

content.explanation: Learn the most common words for clothes in English.
Colours are adjectives — they come BEFORE the noun they describe.

items.note: bright red = vivid, pale yellow = soft

content.word_order: colour + noun: a red shirt, blue jeans, a black jacket

content.explanation: Use 'wearing' (present continuous) to describe clothes right now.

examples: She is wearing a red dress.
He is wearing jeans and a white shirt.
She is wearing a dark blue jacket.

content.instruction: Repeat these clothing description sentences.

phrases.text: She is wearing a bright red saree.
He is wearing dark blue jeans and a white shirt.
What colour is your jacket?
I'm wearing a light grey sweater today.
Those shoes are beautiful!

content.question: Which phrase has the correct word order?
content.explanation: Colour comes BEFORE the noun: a + colour + noun = a blue shirt. ✓

content.question: You wear these on your feet when it is very cold outside.
content.explanation: Boots are warm, strong shoes for cold weather.

content.question: Complete: 'Look at Maya — she ___ a beautiful green dress today!'
content.explanation: Right now (Look!) → present continuous. She is wearing. ✓

content.sentence: She is wearing a ___ blue jacket — not dark, quite soft and light.
content.explanation: Light blue = soft, pale shade of blue — not dark, not very vivid.

tasks.title: Fill in is wearing / are wearing

content.sentence: He ___ a grey suit and she ___ a red blouse with black trousers.
content.explanation: He/She → is wearing (present continuous third person singular).

content.instruction: Rearrange to describe what someone is wearing.
content.correct_sentence: She is wearing a white dress and gold sandals.
content.explanation: She + is wearing + a + colour + clothing + and + colour + clothing.

content.instruction: Match each clothing item or outfit to the most suitable occasion.

tasks.title: Dialogue — Getting dressed for a party

content.context: Nisha is getting ready for a party. She asks her sister for advice.

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: I don't know what to wear to the party tonight!

dialogue.speaker: Sister
dialogue.line: What about that blue dress? It looks beautiful on you.

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: The dark blue one or the light blue one?

dialogue.speaker: Sister
dialogue.line: The dark blue one. Wear it with your gold sandals.

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Good idea! And should I wear a scarf?

dialogue.speaker: Sister
dialogue.line: Yes — your white scarf will look great with it.

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Perfect! What are you wearing?

dialogue.speaker: Sister
dialogue.line: I'm wearing my red dress with black heels.

dialogue.speaker: Nisha
dialogue.line: Oh, that's a great outfit! We'll both look amazing!

comprehension_questions.question: Which dress does the sister recommend?
What shoes does the sister suggest with the dress?
What is the sister wearing to the party?
options: A red dress with black heels

content.instruction: Choose the correct sentence.
content.wrong_sentence: He is wearing a jacket black and trouser blue.
options: He is wearing a jacket black and trouser blue.
He is wearing a black jacket and blue trousers.
He wear a black jacket and blue trousers.
He wearing black jacket and blue trouser.
content.explanation: Colour BEFORE noun. 'Trousers' is plural. He is wearing. ✓

content.scenario: A friend calls and asks what you are wearing for the office today.
content.question: Which is the most natural reply?
options: I wear blue jeans and shirt white.
I am wearing navy blue trousers and a white shirt.
I wearing navy trouser and white.
I have blue trouser and shirt.
content.explanation: Present continuous (wearing), colour before noun, correct plural 'trousers'. ✓

content.instruction: Speak for 45 seconds. Describe what you are wearing today AND describe one other person's outfit (real or imaginary).
content.prompt: What are you wearing right now? Also describe someone else's clothes.
content.example_response: Today I am wearing dark blue jeans and a light grey t-shirt. I also have white trainers. It's a casual day so I dressed comfortably. My colleague is wearing a formal black suit with a white shirt and a dark red tie. He looks very professional! My manager is wearing a beautiful purple dress with black heels.

what_you_learned: Clothes: shirt, jeans, dress, suit, jacket, coat, scarf, shoes, boots, sandals
Colours: red, blue, green, yellow, black, white, grey, pink, light/dark + colour
Word order: colour + noun (a blue shirt, dark jeans)
Describing: He/She is wearing + colour + clothing
Present continuous for clothes: is wearing / are wearing

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn to express emotions, ask how people feel, and understand emotional vocabulary.

content.explanation: These words describe how a person feels inside.

tasks.title: Expressing feelings — I feel / I am

content.explanation: Two patterns to express how you feel.

examples: I feel happy because it's my birthday.
I am nervous because I have an exam tomorrow.
She feels sad because her friend is leaving.

tasks.title: Intensifiers — very, really, a bit, quite

content.explanation: Add these words before the emotion to show HOW MUCH you feel it.

items.note: Strong feeling — I am very happy.
Strong — spoken English — I feel really tired.
Small feeling — I feel a bit nervous.

content.instruction: Repeat with natural emotion — try to FEEL what you say!

phrases.text: I'm really excited about the trip!
I feel a bit nervous before the exam.
She looks very upset today.
I'm absolutely thrilled — I got the job!
Why do you look so worried?

content.question: You have a job interview in one hour. How do you probably feel?
content.explanation: Before important events like interviews, people usually feel nervous.

content.question: Both 'I feel happy' and 'I am happy' are correct — but which intensifier fits best?
options: I feel a bit happy because it's Friday! ✓ / I feel absolutely bored ✓
I feel very happy / I am really excited ✓
I feel happy I / I am happy I ✓
I feeling happy / I being happy ✓
content.explanation: Both 'I feel very happy' and 'I am really excited' are correct — intensifier before adjective.

content.question: Complete: 'I feel sad ___ my friend is moving to another city.'
content.explanation: 'Because' introduces a reason. I feel sad BECAUSE...

content.sentence: She passed all her exams! She is absolutely ___.
content.explanation: Passing exams is a great result → absolutely thrilled (extremely happy).

content.sentence: I am ___ nervous — just a little. I'll be fine after the first five minutes.
content.explanation: 'Just a little' tells us the feeling is small → a bit nervous.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct sentence about feelings with a reason.
content.correct_sentence: I am really excited because my holiday starts tomorrow.
content.explanation: I am + intensifier + emotion + because + reason.

content.instruction: Match each situation to the most likely emotion.

pairs.prompt: You get a surprise birthday party
You have to speak in front of 100 people
You finish a very hard project at work
Someone takes your things without asking

content.context: Kavya is about to do her first presentation at work. Her manager Suresh talks to her.

dialogue.speaker: Suresh
dialogue.line: Kavya, are you okay? You look a bit pale.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.line: I'm a bit nervous, sir. It's my first big presentation.

dialogue.speaker: Suresh
dialogue.line: That's completely normal! How are you feeling about the content?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.line: I feel quite confident about the slides — I worked really hard.

dialogue.speaker: Suresh
dialogue.line: That's great. Are you worried about the questions at the end?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.line: Yes, a little. I'm scared I won't know all the answers.

dialogue.speaker: Suresh
dialogue.line: Don't worry — if you don't know, just say 'That's a great question, let me check and come back to you.'

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.line: Oh! That's really helpful. I feel much better now, thank you!

dialogue.speaker: Suresh
dialogue.line: You'll be brilliant. Good luck!

comprehension_questions.question: How does Kavya feel at the beginning?
What is Kavya scared about?
How does Kavya feel at the end of the conversation?

content.instruction: Which sentence is correct?
content.wrong_sentence: I am feel very nervous because my exam is tomorrow.
options: I am feel very nervous because my exam is tomorrow.
I feel very nervous because my exam is tomorrow.
I feeling very nervous because my exam is tomorrow.
I very nervous feel because my exam tomorrow.
content.explanation: Use 'I feel' OR 'I am' — never 'I am feel'. I feel very nervous. ✓

content.scenario: Your friend tells you: 'I didn't get the job I wanted.'
content.question: What is the most empathetic natural response?
options: Oh no, you must be really disappointed. Are you okay?
Why did you not get it?
content.explanation: Empathetic response: name the emotion (disappointed) + ask how they are.

content.instruction: Speak for 45 seconds. Describe 3 different feelings you have had recently. Give a reason for each.
content.prompt: Tell me about your feelings this week. How did you feel on different days? Why?
content.example_response: On Monday I felt really stressed because I had a lot of work and a deadline. On Wednesday I was quite happy because I finished a big project and my manager was pleased. On Thursday I was a bit nervous because I had to speak in a team meeting. Yesterday I felt very relaxed because it was a quiet day. Today I feel quite excited because it's the weekend tomorrow!

what_you_learned: Emotions: happy, excited, proud, sad, angry, nervous, worried, scared, bored, confused
Patterns: I feel + adjective / I am + adjective
Reasons: I feel ___ because ___
Intensifiers: very, really (strong) / quite (moderate) / a bit, a little (small)
Asking: How do you feel? / Are you okay? / You look ___.

content_manifest.grammar_point: Expand your adjective vocabulary with opposites, learn to use intensifiers, and practise describing things.

content.explanation: Knowing opposite pairs doubles your vocabulary instantly.
Place these before an adjective to show the strength of the description.
content.rule: Intensifier always comes BEFORE the adjective: very + old ✓, old very ✗

tasks.title: Adjective position — before noun OR after 'to be'

content.explanation: Adjectives can go in two positions in a sentence.

content.instruction: Repeat these descriptive sentences. Focus on the stress on the intensifier.

phrases.text: This coffee is absolutely delicious.
The exam was really difficult.
It's quite cold today — not very cold, just a bit chilly.
The traffic is extremely noisy outside.
My bag is a bit heavy — can you help?

content.question: What is the opposite of 'expensive'?
content.explanation: Expensive ↔ cheap. A cheap price is low. An expensive price is high.

content.question: Which sentence has the adjective in the correct position?
content.explanation: Article + adjective + noun: a + tall + building. ✓

content.question: The room is 40 degrees. Which intensifier fits best?
content.explanation: 40 degrees is extreme heat → extremely hot or absolutely boiling.

content.sentence: The first question was easy but the last one was very ___.
content.explanation: Easy ↔ difficult. The last question was very difficult.

content.sentence: The hotel was ___ expensive — it cost 20,000 rupees per night!
content.explanation: 20,000 rupees per night is extreme → extremely expensive.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct descriptive sentence.
content.correct_sentence: The restaurant is really expensive but the food is very delicious.
content.explanation: subject + is + intensifier + adjective + but + subject + is + intensifier + adjective.

content.instruction: Match each adjective to its opposite.

content.context: Arjun just moved to a new flat. He tells his friend Meera about it.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: So how's the new flat? Is it nice?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.line: It's quite nice, actually! The rooms are really big and clean.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: What about the area?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.line: The area is a bit noisy — there's a busy road outside. But it's very convenient.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: Is it far from work?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.line: No — it's quite close. Only about fifteen minutes by bus.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: And the rent? Is it expensive?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.line: It's a bit expensive — but not extremely. For the space and location, it's a good price.

dialogue.speaker: Meera
dialogue.line: Sounds great! I'll come and visit soon.

comprehension_questions.question: How does Arjun describe the rooms?
What is one problem with the area?
How long is the commute to work?

content.instruction: Choose the correct sentence.
content.wrong_sentence: The bag is heavy very and expensive quite.
options: The bag is heavy very and expensive quite.
The bag is very heavy and quite expensive.
Very the bag is heavy and quite expensive.
The bag very is heavy and expensive quite.
content.explanation: Intensifier goes BEFORE the adjective: very heavy, quite expensive. ✓

tasks.title: Speaking — Describe your home or workplace

content.instruction: Speak for 45 seconds. Describe a place you know well using adjectives and intensifiers.
content.prompt: Describe your home, office, or school. Use at least 6 adjectives with intensifiers.
content.example_response: My home is quite small but very clean and comfortable. The kitchen is a bit old but it's very functional. My bedroom is quite quiet — I like it because I sleep very well. The area is a bit noisy during the day because of traffic, but it's extremely quiet at night. The building is quite old — about 30 years — but the rooms are really big. I'm very happy there.
key_phrases: very / really / quite / a bit / extremely + adjective

what_you_learned: Opposite pairs: big/small, fast/slow, hot/cold, expensive/cheap, easy/difficult, clean/dirty, full/empty
Intensifiers scale: a bit → quite → very/really → absolutely/extremely
Intensifier always BEFORE adjective: very cold ✓, cold very ✗
Two positions: a tall building (before noun) / The building is tall (after 'to be')

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn rooms of a house, furniture vocabulary, and prepositions of place to describe where things are.

content.explanation: Learn the names for every room in a house or flat.

items.note: Room with only a toilet (British English)
Outside area attached to the flat

content.explanation: Learn what is inside each room.
Use these words to say WHERE things and people are.

items.note: Inside: The milk is in the fridge.
On a surface: The book is on the table.
Below: The shoes are under the bed.
Right beside: The lamp is next to the bed.
In the middle of two things: The sofa is between the two chairs.
Facing: The TV is opposite the sofa.
At the back: The garden is behind the house.
Higher/lower than: The mirror is above the sink.

content.instruction: Repeat these sentences about home and furniture clearly.

phrases.text: The sofa is in the living room, opposite the TV.
My keys are on the table next to the door.
The bathroom is between the bedroom and the kitchen.
The fridge is in the kitchen, next to the cooker.
There is a mirror above the sink.

content.question: Where do you sleep in a house?
content.explanation: The bedroom is the room for sleeping.

content.question: 'The cat is sleeping ___ the sofa.' (The cat is on top of the sofa.)
content.explanation: On a surface → on. The cat is on the sofa.

content.question: 'My shoes are ___ the bed.' (They are below the bed, not visible.)
content.explanation: Below the bed → under. My shoes are under the bed.

content.sentence: The lamp is ___ the table, ___ the two chairs.
content.explanation: on the table (surface), between the two chairs (in the middle).

content.sentence: I keep my clothes in the ___ in my bedroom — it has two big doors.
content.explanation: A wardrobe is a large piece of bedroom furniture for clothes with doors.

content.instruction: Rearrange to describe where something is.
content.correct_sentence: The mirror is above the sink.
content.explanation: The + [item] + is + [preposition] + the + [reference object].

content.instruction: Match each item of furniture to the room where you usually find it.

tasks.title: Dialogue — Showing a new flatmate around

content.context: Sara is showing her new flatmate Ahmed around their flat for the first time.

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: Welcome! Come in — I'll show you around the flat.

dialogue.speaker: Ahmed
dialogue.line: Thank you! It looks nice. Is this the living room?

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: Yes! The sofa is in front of the TV and there's a small coffee table in the middle.

dialogue.speaker: Ahmed
dialogue.line: It's really comfortable looking. Where's the kitchen?

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: It's just down the hallway — on the left. The fridge is next to the cooker.

dialogue.speaker: Ahmed
dialogue.line: And my bedroom?

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: Your room is at the end of the corridor. There's a wardrobe and a desk inside.

dialogue.speaker: Ahmed
dialogue.line: Is there a mirror in the room?

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.line: Yes — it's above the chest of drawers. And the bathroom is between your room and mine.

dialogue.speaker: Ahmed
dialogue.line: Perfect. It all looks great — I love it!

comprehension_questions.question: Where is the kitchen?
options: Down the hallway on the left

comprehension_questions.question: Where is Ahmed's bedroom?
options: At the end of the corridor

comprehension_questions.question: Where is the mirror in Ahmed's room?

content.instruction: Which sentence uses the preposition correctly?
content.wrong_sentence: The lamp is in the table next to the window.
options: The lamp is in the table next to the window.
The lamp is on the table next to the window.
The lamp is above the table next to the window.
The lamp is under the table next to the window.
content.explanation: A lamp sits ON a table (on a surface). Not 'in the table'. ✓

content.instruction: Speak for 55 seconds. Describe your home — rooms, furniture, and where things are. Use at least 5 prepositions of place.
content.prompt: Describe your home. How many rooms are there? What is in each room? Where are things?
content.example_response: My home is a two-bedroom flat. The living room is quite big — there is a large sofa in front of the TV and a coffee table between the sofa and the wall. The kitchen is next to the living room. The fridge is in the corner, next to the cooker. My bedroom has a big wardrobe opposite the bed and a lamp on the small table beside my bed. There is a mirror above the chest of drawers. The bathroom is between the two bedrooms. We also have a small balcony behind the living room with some plants.
key_phrases: in / on / under / next to / between / opposite / above / behind

what_you_learned: Rooms: bedroom, living room, kitchen, bathroom, hallway, dining room, study, balcony
Furniture: bed, wardrobe, sofa, armchair, fridge, cooker, bookshelf, curtains, mirror
Prepositions: in, on, under, next to, between, opposite, above, behind
on = surface, in = enclosed space, under = below, between = in the middle

content_manifest.grammar_point: Learn animals and nature vocabulary, describe animals, and use 'can' for ability in natural contexts.

content.explanation: Learn common animals and where they live.
Words for the natural world around us.

items.note: Area with many trees — jungle is thick and tropical
Moving freshwater — flows to the sea
Dry, sandy area with very little rain

content.explanation: Use 'can' to describe what animals are able to do.

examples.sentence: An eagle can fly very high.
A fish can swim but it can't walk.
Elephants can swim — did you know that?
A cheetah can run very fast.

interesting_facts: Dolphins can communicate with each other.
Owls can see in the dark.
Peacocks can open their feathers like a fan.
Tigers can't purr but lions can roar very loudly.

content.instruction: Repeat these nature and animal sentences.

phrases.text: Tigers live in the jungle and can swim very well.
Dolphins are intelligent and can communicate.
The river flows from the mountains to the sea.
Eagles can fly very high in the sky.
My favourite animal is the elephant.

content.question: Where does a dolphin live?
content.explanation: Dolphins are sea creatures — they live in the ocean.

content.question: 'A bat ___ see well in the dark.' Complete correctly.
content.explanation: Can + base verb. A bat can see in the dark. ✓

content.question: Which of these is a pet animal (kept at home)?
content.explanation: Rabbits are common pets kept at home. The others are wild animals.

content.sentence: Fish ___ swim but they ___ walk or breathe on land.
content.explanation: Fish can swim ✓. Fish can't walk or breathe on land ✓.

content.sentence: India has many large ___s like the Ganges and the Brahmaputra that flow across the country.
content.explanation: The Ganges and Brahmaputra are famous rivers in India.

content.instruction: Rearrange to make a correct animal fact sentence.
content.correct_sentence: Elephants can swim but can't fly.
content.explanation: Subject + can + verb + but + can't + verb.

content.instruction: Match each animal to where it lives.

content.context: A family visits the zoo. Little Aryan asks his mother questions about the animals.

dialogue.speaker: Aryan
dialogue.line: Mum! Look at that big animal! What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Mother
dialogue.line: That's an elephant, Aryan. Isn't it beautiful?

dialogue.speaker: Aryan
dialogue.line: It's huge! Can elephants swim?

dialogue.speaker: Mother
dialogue.line: Yes! Elephants can swim very well. They love water.

dialogue.speaker: Aryan
dialogue.line: Wow! Can they run fast?

dialogue.speaker: Mother
dialogue.line: They can run — but not as fast as a cheetah. Cheetahs are the fastest land animals.

dialogue.speaker: Aryan
dialogue.line: What about those birds over there? Can they fly?

dialogue.speaker: Mother
dialogue.line: Those are peacocks. They can fly a little but they don't fly very high or far.

dialogue.speaker: Aryan
dialogue.line: And can peacocks talk like parrots?

dialogue.speaker: Mother
dialogue.line: No, they can't talk — but they can make a very loud call! Let's go and see the tigers next.

comprehension_questions.question: Can elephants swim?
Which is the fastest land animal according to the mother?
Can peacocks talk like parrots?

content.instruction: Which sentence about animals is correct?
content.wrong_sentence: A tiger can swims and climb trees.
options: A tiger can swims and climb trees.
A tiger can swim and climb trees.
A tiger cans swim and can climb trees.
A tiger can swimming and climbing trees.
content.explanation: Can + BASE verb (no -s, no -ing). A tiger can swim and climb. ✓

content.instruction: Speak for 45 seconds. Describe your favourite animal. Use can/can't and adjective vocabulary.
content.prompt: What is your favourite animal? Describe it — what it looks like, where it lives, what it can do.
content.example_response: My favourite animal is the tiger. Tigers are big, powerful, and beautiful. They live in forests and jungles in Asia. A tiger can swim very well — it loves water! It can also run very fast — up to 65 kilometres per hour. Tigers are mostly orange with black stripes. They are strong and quiet hunters. Unfortunately, tigers are endangered — there are not many left in the wild. I think we should protect them.

what_you_learned: Animal groups: pets, farm animals, wild animals, birds, water animals
Nature: forest, ocean, river, lake, mountain, beach, desert
Can/can't for animal abilities: A fish can swim. An eagle can fly.
Animal descriptions: size, colour, habitat, special abilities

content_manifest.grammar_point: Review Unit 3: body, health, doctor, clothes, feelings, adjectives, home, and animals.

content.explanation: Quick recap of all Unit 3 lessons.

summary.topic: Body parts + health: I have a headache / My knee hurts / I feel sick
Clothes + colours: colour + noun word order, is wearing
Feelings: I feel/am + adjective, because, intensifiers
Adjective opposites + intensifiers: very/quite/a bit + adjective
Home: rooms, furniture, prepositions of place (on/in/under/between)
Animals + nature: can/can't for ability, habitats

content.question: Which sentence about health is correct?
options: I have headache and my stomach hurt.
I have a headache and my stomach hurts.
I have a headache and my stomach hurt.
I am headache and stomach hurts.
content.explanation: I have a headache (article 'a'). My stomach hurts (singular → hurts). ✓

content.question: 'I have had a cold ___ Monday' and 'for ___ three days'.
content.explanation: Monday = specific time → since. Three days = duration → for.

content.question: Which is the correct description of someone's outfit?
options: She wearing dress red and shoes black.
She is wearing a red dress and black shoes.
She wear red dress and black shoe.
She is wearing dress red and shoes black.
content.explanation: She is wearing + a + colour + noun. Colour before noun. Plural shoes. ✓

content.question: Complete: 'I feel ___ nervous — just a small feeling, not a lot.'
content.explanation: Small feeling → a bit. I feel a bit nervous. ✓

content.question: What is the opposite of 'noisy'?
content.explanation: Noisy ↔ quiet.

content.question: 'The book is ___ the table.' (It is on top of the table.)
content.explanation: On top of a surface → on. The book is on the table.

content.question: Which sentence about animals is correct?
options: Eagles can to fly very high.
content.explanation: Can + base verb. Eagles can fly. ✓ (No -s, no 'to', no -ing)

content.sentence: The doctor said I ___ rest and I ___ eat spicy food for three days.
content.explanation: Rest = positive advice → should. Avoid spicy food = negative advice → shouldn't.

content.sentence: The lamp is ___ the desk and the bed is ___ the wardrobe and the window.
content.explanation: on the desk (surface), between (in the middle of two things).

content.sentence: She passed her driving test! She is absolutely ___ and very ___.
content.explanation: Passing a test = thrilled (extremely happy) and proud (good feeling about achievement).

content.instruction: Rearrange all words to make one correct sentence.
content.correct_sentence: I have a sore throat and a fever since yesterday.
content.explanation: I have + a + [problem] + and + a + [problem] + since + [time].

content.instruction: This message has 4 mistakes. Which version corrects them all?
content.wrong_text: Hi, I feel very bad today. I have headache since three days. My doctor say I should to rest. I am wearing pyjamas blue and sitting in my sofa.
options: Hi, I feel very bad today. I have a headache for three days. My doctor says I should rest. I am wearing blue pyjamas and sitting on my sofa.
Hi, I feel very bad today. I have headache for three days. My doctor say I should rest. I am wearing blue pyjamas and sitting in my sofa.
Hi, I feel very bad today. I have a headache since three days. My doctor says I should to rest. I am wearing pyjamas blue and sitting on my sofa.
Hi, I feel very bad today. I have a headache for three days. My doctor says I should rest. I am wearing pyjamas blue and sitting in my sofa.
content.explanation: 4 errors: 'a headache' (article), 'for' not 'since' (duration), 'says' (3rd person), 'should rest' (no 'to'), 'blue pyjamas' (colour before noun), 'on my sofa' (on surface not 'in').

tasks.title: Final dialogue — A phone call catching up

content.context: Old friends Vikram and Ananya haven't spoken in a while. They catch up on the phone.

dialogue.speaker: Vikram
dialogue.line: Ananya! How are you? It's been so long!

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Vikram! I'm really happy you called. I've been quite busy.

dialogue.speaker: Vikram
dialogue.line: Are you okay? You sound a bit tired.

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: I was sick last week — I had a bad cold and a fever for about four days.

dialogue.speaker: Vikram
dialogue.line: Oh no! Are you feeling better now?

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Yes, much better! The doctor said I should rest and drink lots of water.

dialogue.speaker: Vikram
dialogue.line: Good. So what else is new? I heard you moved to a new flat!

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Yes! It's really nice — quite big, very clean, and extremely quiet.

dialogue.speaker: Vikram
dialogue.line: What's your favourite room?

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Definitely the living room — there's a huge sofa in front of the window and I can see trees and birds outside.

dialogue.speaker: Vikram
dialogue.line: That sounds perfect! I'm a bit jealous honestly.

dialogue.speaker: Ananya
dialogue.line: Come and visit! I'll show you around.

How does Ananya describe her new flat?

tasks.title: Final speaking — Part 1: Health and feelings

content.instruction: Speak for 50 seconds combining health and feelings vocabulary.
content.prompt: Describe a time you were sick. How did you feel? What did the doctor say? How do you feel now?
content.example_response: Last month I felt absolutely terrible. I had a high fever and a really bad headache for about five days. I also had a sore throat and a cough. I felt very weak and quite dizzy. I went to the doctor. She asked me how long I had felt sick — I told her since the previous Monday. She said I should rest at home, drink lots of water, and take tablets three times a day. She also said I shouldn't go to work. After a week I felt much better — quite relieved and grateful! Now I feel completely fine.

tasks.title: Final speaking — Part 2: Describe your world

content.instruction: Speak for 60 seconds. Describe your home AND your favourite animal. Use adjectives, prepositions, and can/can't.
content.prompt: Tell me about your home — rooms, furniture, how things are arranged. Then describe your favourite animal.
content.example_response: My home is a three-bedroom flat on the fourth floor. The living room is quite large — there's a big sofa opposite the TV and a coffee table in the middle. The kitchen is very clean and organised — the fridge is next to the cooker and there are cupboards above the sink. My bedroom has a wardrobe beside the door and a lamp on the table next to my bed. It's very comfortable. My favourite animal is the tiger. It's absolutely beautiful — large, powerful, and elegant. Tigers live in forests and can swim very well. They can also run very fast. I feel very sad that they are endangered.

content.congratulations: Amazing work! You have completed Unit 3. You can now talk about your health, visit a doctor, describe clothes and colours, express your feelings, describe your home, and talk about animals — all in English!

Colour + noun word order (a blue shirt)
I feel / I am + adjective + because ___
Intensifiers: a bit → quite → very/really → absolutely/extremely

units.title: Family & Describing People
units.description: Talk about family members and describe people's appearance.

content_data.text: Family words: mother (mom), father (dad), brother, sister, grandfather, grandmother, uncle, aunt, cousin. 'This is my ___.'
examples: I have two brothers and one sister.

content_data.text: Extended family: uncle (parent's brother), aunt (parent's sister), cousin (uncle/aunt's child), nephew, niece.
examples: I visit my aunt on weekends.

content_data.text: Practice introducing your family.
content_data.prompt: Say: 'I have a big family. My father is [job]. My mother is [job]. I have [number] brothers and [number] sisters.'
content_data.tip: Use 'have' with I/you/we/they. Use 'has' with he/she/it.
content_data.model_answer: I have a big family. My father is an engineer. My mother is a teacher. I have one brother and two sisters.

content_data.text: Describe how people look: tall/short, young/old, thin/fat. Hair: long/short, black/brown/blonde. Eyes: brown/blue/green. 'He is tall. She has long black hair.'
examples: My sister has long black hair.

content_data.text: Test your family and description vocabulary.
Describe personality: kind, happy, funny, friendly, quiet, smart, brave, shy, honest, lazy.
examples: My father is kind and honest.

content_data.text: Practice pronouncing family and description words.
content_data.prompt: Repeat: Mother. Father. Brother. Sister. Tall. Short. Young. Old. Kind. Funny. Smart.
content_data.tip: 'Brother' — the 'th' sound needs tongue between teeth. Practice: bro-THER.
content_data.model_answer: Mother. Father. Brother. Sister. Tall. Short. Young. Old. Kind. Funny. Smart.

content_data.text:
Read about Anita's family:

My name is Anita. I have a big family. My father is tall and kind. He is a doctor. My mother is short and funny. She is a teacher. I have one brother — his name is Rahul. He is 15 years old and very smart. My sister Priya is 8. She is shy but very kind. My grandmother lives with us. She is old but very happy.
examples: My father is tall and kind.
My mother is short and funny.
He is 15 years old and very smart.
She is shy but very kind.
